tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Identify the three label/selector fields that must be mutually consistent in a Deployment manifest to ensure the Deployment correctly manages its Pods. Given the partial manifest below, fill in the two blank labels so that the Deployment can find and manage the Pods it creates.
tasks.starter:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
  labels:
    app: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ________        # blank A — the label the Deployment looks for on its Pods
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ________      # blank B — the label stamped on each Pod
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-app
          image: nginx:1.25
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
tasks.solution:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
  labels:
    app: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app        # blank A — must match the Pod template labels
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app      # blank B — must match the selector above
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-app
          image: nginx:1.25
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80
tasks.explanation: A Deployment's 'selector.matchLabels' is a query: it finds all Pods that carry exactly those labels and claims them as its own. The 'template.metadata.labels' is what the Deployment stamps onto every Pod it creates. For the Deployment to manage its Pods, these two must match. If they differ, the Deployment either adopts the wrong Pods or finds none of its own — Kubernetes will actually refuse to create the Deployment and return a validation error. Both blanks must therefore be 'app: my-app'. The top-level 'metadata.labels' on the Deployment itself is separate — it labels the Deployment object, not the Pods.
hints: If selector.matchLabels and template.metadata.labels do not match, 'kubectl apply' returns an error: 'selector does not match template labels'.
You can use multiple labels in a selector (e.g., app: my-app AND tier: backend) for finer-grained grouping, but all must appear on the Pods.
kubectl describe deployment my-app shows the selector and controlled Pods — useful for verifying the match after applying.

tasks.prompt: Core practice A — Write a complete Deployment manifest for your own application. Use the following specifications: app name 'web-api', 3 replicas, ECR image '111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/web-api:abc1234', container port 8080. Include all four required top-level fields (apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec). Substitute your own account ID, region, repo name, and commit tag where appropriate.
tasks.starter:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: ________
kind: ________
metadata:
  name: ________
  labels:
    app: ________
spec:
  replicas: ________
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ________
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ________
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: ________
          image: ________
          ports:
            - containerPort: ________
tasks.solution:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-api
  labels:
    app: web-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-api
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web-api
          image: 111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/web-api:abc1234
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
tasks.explanation: Every Kubernetes object shares the same four top-level fields: apiVersion (which API version to use — Deployments use 'apps/v1'), kind (the object type), metadata (name and labels to identify the object), and spec (the desired state). For Deployments the critical spec fields are: replicas (how many Pod copies), selector (how to find owned Pods), and template (the Pod blueprint, which must carry labels matching the selector). The image line — ending in ':abc1234' — is the injection point where the pipeline inserts each new commit tag; only this one value changes between deployments.
hints: apiVersion for core objects (Pod, Service, ConfigMap) is 'v1'; for Deployments it is 'apps/v1'. Check 'kubectl api-resources' to see all supported kinds and their apiVersions.
containerPort is documentation — it does not actually publish the port. The Service manifest (next task) does the actual routing.
After writing the file, validate it without applying: kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client

tasks.prompt: Core practice B — Write a Service manifest that exposes the 'web-api' Deployment to the public internet via an AWS load balancer. The Service should route traffic from port 80 (external) to port 8080 (container). Apply both manifests to your cluster using kubectl and confirm the Pods are running.
tasks.starter:
# service.yaml
apiVersion: ________
kind: ________
metadata:
  name: web-api
spec:
  type: ________         # choose the type that provisions an AWS load balancer
  selector:
    app: ________        # must match the Deployment's Pod labels
  ports:
    - port: ________     # external port clients connect to
      targetPort: ________ # port on the container

---
# Commands to apply and verify:
# kubectl apply -f ________
# kubectl apply -f ________
# kubectl get pods
# kubectl get svc web-api
tasks.solution:
# service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-api
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: web-api
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

---
# Apply manifests:
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f service.yaml

# Verify Pods are running (should show 3 Running pods):
kubectl get pods

# Get the load balancer external IP/hostname:
kubectl get svc web-api
tasks.explanation: A Service with 'type: LoadBalancer' on EKS causes AWS to provision an Elastic Load Balancer (ELB/NLB) automatically and assign it a public DNS hostname. The Service's 'selector' field uses the same label ('app: web-api') that the Deployment stamps on its Pods — this is the label glue that connects Service → Pods without hard-coding Pod IPs. 'port: 80' is the port clients hit on the load balancer; 'targetPort: 8080' is the port traffic is forwarded to on each Pod. The 'kubectl get svc web-api' output will show an EXTERNAL-IP (or hostname) once the AWS load balancer is provisioned, which may take 30–90 seconds.
hints: The EXTERNAL-IP column shows '<pending>' until the AWS load balancer is fully provisioned. Wait ~1 minute and re-run kubectl get svc.
For an internal-only service (not exposed to the internet), use 'type: ClusterIP' — it is the default and gives a stable cluster-internal IP.
Confirm the Service is routing to the right Pods with: kubectl describe svc web-api — look at the 'Endpoints' field, which lists Pod IP:port pairs.

tasks.prompt: Applied challenge — Your colleague updated the Deployment to use image tag 'abc5678' but mistakenly used 'app: web-backend' in the Service selector, while the Deployment still labels its Pods 'app: web-api'. Diagnose the problem using kubectl commands and fix both the label mismatch and the image tag. Show the corrected service.yaml and the kubectl command to update only the image tag in the running Deployment without editing the file.
tasks.starter:
# Broken service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-api
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: web-backend   # <-- BUG: does not match Deployment Pod labels
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

# Step 1: Diagnose — which kubectl command shows the Service has no endpoints?
# kubectl ________

# Step 2: Fix the Service selector in service.yaml and re-apply.
# (Write the corrected service.yaml)

# Step 3: Update only the image tag in the running Deployment to 'abc5678'
# without editing deployment.yaml.
# kubectl ________
tasks.solution:
# Step 1: Diagnose — describe the Service to see Endpoints
kubectl describe svc web-api
# The 'Endpoints' field will show '<none>' if the selector finds no Pods.
# Also helpful:
kubectl get endpoints web-api
# Compare to actual Pod labels:
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# Step 2: Corrected service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-api
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: web-api    # fixed: matches Deployment Pod label
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080

# Re-apply the fixed Service:
kubectl apply -f service.yaml

# Step 3: Update only the image tag with kubectl set image
# Format: kubectl set image deployment/<deployment-name> <container-name>=<new-image>
kubectl set image deployment/web-api web-api=111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/web-api:abc5678
tasks.explanation: When a Service selector does not match any Pod labels, 'kubectl describe svc' shows 'Endpoints: <none>' — traffic hits the load balancer but has nowhere to go, causing connection errors. The fix is to align the selector with the actual Pod labels. 'kubectl get pods --show-labels' shows what labels the running Pods carry; compare these to the Service selector. For the image update, 'kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container-name>=<image>:<tag>' updates the image of a named container in the Deployment in place. The container name ('web-api' after the =) must match the 'name:' field under 'containers:' in the Deployment spec. This triggers a rolling update immediately, but note that the deployment.yaml file on disk still has the old tag — for keeping the file and cluster in sync, prefer editing the file and running 'kubectl apply -f'.
hints: 'kubectl get endpoints web-api' shows the Pod IPs currently backing the Service. If empty, the selector is the likely culprit.
kubectl get pods --show-labels outputs all Pod labels in a readable column — compare these directly to the Service selector.
After running 'kubectl set image', watch the rollout: kubectl rollout status deployment/web-api
